epithet: Father of Dragons / the Golden / first of the Uruloki
end: Slain by Turin Turambar, who drove Gurthang into his soft belly from the gorge of Cabed-en-Aras; the Dragon's death-throe and dying glance destroyed Nienor and then Turin himself.
q: Again after a hundred years Glaurung, the first of the Urulóki, the fire-drakes of the North, issued from Angband’s gates by night.
note: Named 'Glaurung the golden, father of dragons' at the Dagor Bragollach (t1_a_the_silmarillion.txt:7520). Dates from the Grey Annals: he first issued in 260 (t1_11_the_war_of_the_jewels.txt:1862) and was slain in 499 (t1_11_the_war_of_the_jewels.txt:3630). In The Book of Lost Tales the same dragon is Glórund (t1_a_the_silmarillion.txt:1234).

epithet: the Black / mightiest of the dragon-host
end: Slain in the air by Earendil before sunrise in the Great Battle and cast from the sky; he fell upon the towers of Thangorodrim and broke them in his ruin.
q: Before the rising of the sun Eärendil slew Ancalagon the Black, the mightiest of the dragon-host, and cast him from the sky; and he fell upon the towers of Thangorodrim, and they were broken in his ruin.
note: The first winged dragon loosed by Morgoth. Date from the revised annals of the War of Wrath, 545-587, with the added entry 'Ancalagon is cast down by Eärendil' (t1_11_the_war_of_the_jewels.txt:12734).

epithet: the great dragon of Ered Mithrin
end: Slain by Fram son of Frumgar of the Eotheod; the Dwarves claimed his hoard and, it is said, slew Fram for the insult of the necklace of Scatha's teeth.
q: Of his son, Fram, they tell that he slew Scatha, the great dragon of Ered Mithrin, and the land had peace from the long-worms afterwards.
note: Undated in the corpus; Frumgar led the Eotheod north in T.A. 1977 (t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:48817), so Scatha fell in the early 2000s of the Third Age. The horn Merry received in Rohan 'came from the hoard of Scatha the Worm' (t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:43751).

epithet: the Magnificent / the Golden / the Chiefest and Greatest of Calamities
end: Shot through the hollow of the left breast by Bard the Bowman with the black arrow as he flew over Esgaroth; he fell and destroyed the town in his ruin.
q: There was a most specially greedy, strong and wicked worm called Smaug.
note: Dates from the Tale of Years: 'Smaug the Dragon descends on Erebor' in 2770 (t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:48881), 'Bard of Esgaroth slays Smaug' in 2941 (t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:48940). Described in his hoard at t1_b_the_hobbit_or_there_and_back_again.txt:6012.

epithet: the Red Maw / Anfauglir, the Jaws of Thirst / Wolf-warden of the gate of Angband
end: Slain by Huan in the Wolf-hunt of Doriath after biting off Beren's hand and swallowing the Silmaril; Mablung cut the jewel from his belly.
q: Carcharoth, the Red Maw, he is named in the tales of those days, and Anfauglir, the Jaws of Thirst.
note: This is the wolf of Angband that took Beren's hand; Silm's index says so outright: 'The great wolf of Angband that bit off the hand of Beren bearing the Silmaril; slain by Huan in Doriath' (t1_a_the_silmarillion.txt:14208). Bred by Morgoth from the whelps of Draugluin's race and fed by his own hand. Year from the Grey Annals (t1_11_the_war_of_the_jewels.txt:2451, 2467).

epithet: lord and sire of the werewolves of Angband
end: Mortally wounded by Huan on the bridge of Tol-in-Gaurhoth; he fled back into the tower and died before Sauron's feet, crying 'Huan is there!'
q: Then Sauron sent Draugluin, a dread beast, old in evil, lord and sire of the werewolves of Angband.
note: His flayed wolf-hame was afterwards worn by Beren to enter Angband (t1_a_the_silmarillion.txt:8656).

epithet: the mightiest werewolf that had yet walked the world
end: Not slain: pinned by the throat by Huan, he shifted from wolf to serpent to his own form, yielded the mastery of his tower to Luthien, and fled in the shape of a vampire.
q: Then Huan sprang. There befell the battle of Huan and Wolf-Sauron, and the howls and baying echoed in the hills, and the watchers on the walls of Ered Wethrin across the valley heard it afar and were dismayed.
note: Not a separate beast but Sauron himself in werewolf shape, named as such by the text; included because the name is given to the wolf-form. He 'took upon himself the form of a werewolf, and made himself the mightiest that had yet walked the world' (t1_a_the_silmarillion.txt:8490).

epithet: the Gloomweaver (Wirilome) / Weaver of Darkness
end: Unrecorded; 'some have said that she ended long ago, when in her uttermost famine she devoured herself at last.'
q: Of the fate of Ungoliant no tale tells. Yet some have said that she ended long ago, when in her uttermost famine she devoured herself at last.
note: Dwelt in Avathar before the Darkening of Valinor; 'The Eldar knew not whence she came' (t1_a_the_silmarillion.txt:4162). She served Melkor at the destruction of the Two Trees but 'had disowned her Master', so her allegiance is her own. The epithet Gloomweaver/Wirilome belongs to the earlier Lost Tales form of her (t1_1_the_book_of_lost_tales_part_one.txt:7739).

epithet: Shelob the Great / Her Ladyship
end: Not slain: pierced by Sting and blinded by the Phial of Galadriel in Samwise's hands, she crawled away, and 'whether she lay long in her lair... this tale does not tell.'
q: But none could rival her, Shelob the Great, last child of Ungoliant to trouble the unhappy world.
note: She had dwelt in Torech Ungol 'in ages past' (t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:33063), before Sauron's return; unallied but tolerated by Mordor. She stung Frodo in the neck at Cirith Ungol.

epithet: Huan of Valinor / the Hound of Valinor
end: Slew Carcharoth in the Wolf-hunt of Doriath and was mortally wounded in the same hour, 'and the venom of Morgoth entered into him'; he spoke for the third time and died beside Beren.
q: Now the chief of the wolf-hounds that followed Celegorm was named Huan. He was not born in Middle-earth, but came from the Blessed Realm; for Oromë had given him to Celegorm long ago in Valinor, and there he had followed the horn of his master, before evil came.
note: Doomed to 'meet death, but not until he encountered the mightiest wolf that would ever walk the world'; permitted to speak with words three times. He overthrew both Draugluin and Sauron in wolf-form.

epithet: the horse of Orome
end: Not stated.
q: Nahar is the name of his horse, white in the sun, and shining silver at night.
note: 'said by the Eldar to be so named on account of his voice' (t1_a_the_silmarillion.txt:15170). His neighing at Cuivienen gave the first warning of Melkor's spies (t1_a_the_silmarillion.txt:3114), and 'the hooves of Nahar was the first light that returned to Valinor' after the Darkening (t1_a_the_silmarillion.txt:4310).

epithet: Mansbane renamed / sire of the mearas
end: Died with or soon after Eorl the Young, who fell in battle in the Wold; 'Felaróf was laid there also' in the first mound of the Kings of the Mark.
q: It was upon Felaróf that Eorl rode to the Field of Celebrant; for that horse proved as long-lived as Men, and so were his descendants.
note: He threw and killed Eorl's father Leod, and was claimed by Eorl as weregild: 'Felaróf I name you' (t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:47798). Field of Celebrant 2510 and Eorl's fall 2545 from the Tale of Years (t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:48856, 48858). Unfinished Tales glosses the name as Anglo-Saxon poetic 'very valiant, very strong' (t1_d_unfinished_tales_of_numenor_and_middle_earth.txt:9898). The name is written with a combining acute in the corpus file.

epithet: chief of the Mearas / the horse of the White Rider
end: Not stated; he bore Gandalf to the Grey Havens at the end of the Third Age.
q: That is Shadowfax. He is the chief of the Mearas, lords of horses, and not even Théoden, King of Rohan, has ever looked on a better.
note: Taken by Gandalf from Theoden in T.A. 3018; the Tale of Years records 'Gandalf having tamed Shadowfax rides from Rohan' (t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:49048). Descended from Felarof: 'the sire of their race was the great horse of Eorl that knew the speech of Men' (t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:19637).

epithet: Lightfoot's foal / faithful servant yet master's bane
end: Killed by a dart on the Pelennor; falling in his death-throe he crushed Theoden beneath him. Men dug him a grave and raised Snowmane's Howe.
q: Faithful servant yet master’s bane, Lightfoot’s foal, swift Snowmane.
note: The epitaph carved 'in the tongues of Gondor and the Mark'. Theoden's own steed: 'The king mounted his horse, Snowmane' (t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:34786).

epithet: sire of Snowmane
end: Not stated.
q: Faithful servant yet master’s bane, Lightfoot’s foal, swift Snowmane.
note: Named nowhere else in the corpus: he exists only as the sire in Snowmane's epitaph. Included because the name is Tolkien's and belongs to an individual horse.

epithet: the horse of Garulf, lent to Aragorn
end: Not stated; left to stray in the fields of Rohan and later ridden again by Aragorn from Dunharrow.
q: Hasufel and Arod, weary but proud, followed their tireless leader, a grey shadow before them hardly to be seen.
note: 'A great dark-grey horse was brought to Aragorn... Hasufel is his name, said Eomer. May he bear you well and to better fortune than Garulf, his late master!' (t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:19821-19824).

epithet: the horse Legolas rode bare-backed
end: Not stated; he balked at the Paths of the Dead but was led through, and bore Legolas and Gimli to the end of the war.
q: and to their wonder Arod was tame and willing beneath him, moving here and there with but a spoken word: such was the Elvish way with all good beasts.
note: Lent by Eomer's eored in Rohan; Legolas 'asked them to take off saddle and rein'. He 'refused the way' at the Door of the Dead (t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:35172).

epithet: Aragorn's own horse, brought from the North
end: Not stated.
q: one stood there without a rider, Aragorn’s own horse that they had brought from the North; Roheryn was his name.
note: Brought to Aragorn at Dunharrow by the Grey Company.

epithet: the pony given to Meriadoc by Theoden
end: Not stated; Merry was left behind with Stybba at Dunharrow and rode to war on Windfola instead.
q: The king mounted his horse, Snowmane, and Merry sat beside him on his pony: Stybba was his name.
note: 'This is no journey for such steeds as Stybba, as I have told you' — Theoden forbidding Merry the Muster (t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:35965).

epithet: the great grey steed of Dernhelm
end: Not slain: he threw Eowyn and Merry in terror at the coming of the Nazgul's Shadow and 'ran wild upon the plain'.
q: Thus it came to pass that when the king set out, before Dernhelm sat Meriadoc the hobbit, and the great grey steed Windfola made little of the burden
note: Carried both Eowyn (as Dernhelm) and Merry to the Pelennor.

epithet: the horse of Eomer
end: Not stated.
q: Gandalf will be at the head with the Lord of the Mark; but Firefoot, my horse, will bear us both, if you will.
note: Named once only, when Eomer offers Gimli a place in the saddle in token of pardon.

epithet: the poor beast brought from Bree
end: Survived. Driven off by the Watcher in the Water at Moria's West-gate, he found his own way back to Bree and returned to the Shire with the hobbits.
q: It was Sam who had insisted on choosing him, declaring that Bill (as he called him) would pine, if he did not come.
note: Bought from Bill Ferny at Bree and named after him by Sam. 'Bill the pony gave a wild neigh of fear, and turned tail and dashed away' at the Doors of Durin (t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:14184); he rejoined them at Bree homeward bound (t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:44537).

epithet: King of Eagles / mightiest of all birds that have ever been
end: Not stated; he was captain of the great birds of heaven at the Great Battle.
q: there flew down from the high airs Thorondor, King of Eagles, mightiest of all birds that have ever been, whose outstretched wings spanned thirty fathoms
note: He bore Fingon to rescue Maedhros, rescued the body of Fingolfin and scarred Morgoth's face (t1_a_the_silmarillion.txt:7652), bore Beren and Luthien from Angband, and led the eagles at the War of Wrath (t1_a_the_silmarillion.txt:11745). His eyries were in the Crissaegrim above Gondolin. Quenya form Sorontar/Sorontur in the earlier texts (t1_10_morgoths_ring.txt:15221).

epithet: the Windlord / swiftest of the Great Eagles
end: Not stated.
q: There came Gwaihir the Windlord, and Landroval his brother, greatest of all the Eagles of the North, mightiest of the descendants of old Thorondor, who built his eyries in the inaccessible peaks of the Encircling Mountains when Middle-earth was young.
note: He bore Gandalf three times: from Orthanc, from Zirakzigil, and from Orodruin. 'Twice you have borne me, Gwaihir my friend' (t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:42439).

epithet: brother of Gwaihir / 'Wide-wing'
end: Not stated.
q: There came Gwaihir the Windlord, and Landroval his brother, greatest of all the Eagles of the North, mightiest of the descendants of old Thorondor
note: Bore Frodo or Sam from the Mountain of Fire: 'down swept Gwaihir, and down came Landroval and Meneldor the swift' (t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:42506). The name is glossed 'Wide-wing', vassal of Thorondor, in the earlier texts (t1_5_the_lost_road_and_other_writings.txt:18818), and appears there as Lhandroval.

epithet: young and swift
end: Not stated.
q: And he lifted up Gandalf and sped away south, and with him went Landroval, and Meneldor young and swift.
note: Chosen for speed to carry Gandalf to Orodruin. A late addition to the text: Christopher Tolkien notes his father 'added a new name, Meneldor' (t1_9_sauron_defeated.txt:1635).

epithet: chief of the great ravens of the Mountain
end: Not stated.
q: I am Roäc son of Carc. Carc is dead, but he was well known to you once.
note: 'an aged raven of great size', 153 years old, blind and bald; he brought Thorin the news of Smaug's death and carried word to Dain of the Iron Hills.

epithet: the raven for whom Ravenhill was named
end: Dead before the Quest of Erebor: 'Carc is dead, but he was well known to you once.'
q: This very height was once named Ravenhill, because there was a wise and famous pair, old Carc and his wife, that lived here above the guard-chamber.
note: Father of Roac. His mate is named only as 'his wife'.

epithet: Woman of Secret Shadow / the bat-messenger of Sauron
end: Not narrated; her bat-fell was taken at Tol-in-Gaurhoth and worn by Luthien to enter Angband, which implies she was killed there.
q: She was the messenger of Sauron, and was wont to fly in vampire’s form to Angband; and her great fingered wings were barbed at each joint’s end with an iron claw.
note: Named in the Silmarillion index as 'Woman of Secret Shadow, the messenger of Sauron from Tol-in-Gaurhoth who took the form of a great bat, and in whose shape Luthien entered Angband' (t1_a_the_silmarillion.txt:15586). Luthien took her name before Morgoth: 'Thuringwethil I am, who cast' (t1_e_beren_and_luthien.txt:4949).

epithet: Eldest of the Ents / Fangorn
end: Not stated; he survived the War of the Ring and the destruction of Isengard.
q: Fangorn is my name according to some, Treebeard others make it. Treebeard will do.
note: One of 'only three... of the first Ents that walked in the woods before the Darkness' (t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:21410). He withholds his true Entish name: 'Now that would be telling!'

epithet: Bregalad, the hasty Ent of the rowan-trees
end: Not stated; he led a company of Ents into Isengard and survived.
q: I am Bregalad, that is Quickbeam in your language.
note: 'it is only a nickname, of course. They have called me that ever since I said yes to an elder Ent before he had finished his question.' Grey-green hair, resonant voice higher and clearer than Treebeard's.

epithet: Finglas, one of the three eldest Ents
end: Not stated; he had grown tree-ish and did not march to Isengard.
q: Leaflock has grown sleepy, almost tree-ish, you might say: he has taken to standing by himself half-asleep all through the summer with the deep grass of the meadows round his knees.
note: Named in Elvish as Finglas: 'Fangorn, Finglas, Fladrif' (t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:21408). 'Covered with leafy hair he is.'

epithet: Fladrif, one of the three eldest Ents
end: Not stated; wounded by Orcs, he withdrew to the high places among the birches and would not come down.
q: Skinbark lived on the mountain-slopes west of Isengard. That is where the worst trouble has been. He was wounded by the Orcs, and many of his folk and his tree-herds have been murdered and destroyed.
note: Named in Elvish as Fladrif (t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:21408).

epithet: a very tall handsome Ent
end: Burned to death at Isengard, caught in a spray of Saruman's liquid fire; his death maddened the Ents.
q: One of them, Beechbone I think he was called, a very tall handsome Ent, got caught in a spray of some liquid fire and burned like a torch: a horrible sight.
note: The only Ent recorded as killed in the War of the Ring; Merry qualifies even the name ('I think he was called').

epithet: the Balrog of Moria / a Balrog of Morgoth
end: Cast down from the peak of Zirakzigil by Gandalf after the Battle of the Peak: 'I threw down my enemy, and he fell from the high place and broke the mountain-side where he smote it in his ruin.'
q: ‘Ai! ai!’ wailed Legolas. ‘A Balrog! A Balrog is come!’ Gimli stared with wide eyes. ‘Durin’s Bane!’ he cried, and letting his axe fall he covered his face.
note: Appendix A: 'Thus they roused from sleep a thing of terror that, flying from Thangorodrim, had lain hidden at the foundations of the earth since the coming of the Host of the West: a Balrog of Morgoth' (t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:48147). Tale of Years: 'A Balrog appears in Moria, and slays Durin VI' in 1980 (t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:48821); Gandalf 'casts down the Balrog, and passes away' on 25 January 3019 (t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:49087).

epithet: Lord of Balrogs / high-captain of Angband
end: Slain by Ecthelion of the Fountain in the square of the King at Gondolin, where 'each slew the other'.
q: Gothmog, Lord of Balrogs, high-captain of Angband, was come; and he drove a dark wedge between the Elvenhosts, surrounding King Fingon, and thrusting Turgon and Húrin aside towards the Fen of Serech.
note: CONFLICT: (1) The Silmarillion index makes him 'slayer of Feanor, Fingon, and Ecthelion' and adds that 'The same name was borne in the Third Age by the Lieutenant of Minas Morgul' (t1_a_the_silmarillion.txt:14740) — that second Gothmog appears at t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:37906. (2) In the Lay of the Children of Hurin the Lord of Balrogs is a different name, Lungorthin (t1_3_the_lays_of_beleriand.txt:4322). (3) The number of Balrogs is contradictory: the Silmarillion has 'a host of Balrogs', but Tolkien wrote in the margin of the Annals of Aman 'There should not be supposed more than say 3 or at most 7 ever existed' (t1_10_morgoths_ring.txt:3173). Fall of Gondolin dated 510 in the late annals (t1_11_the_war_of_the_jewels.txt:12721).

epithet: the many-armed thing in the pool before the West-gate
end: Not stated; it sealed the Doors of Durin behind the Company and was not seen again.
q: the pool is up to the wall at Westgate. The Watcher in the Water took Oin. We cannot get out.
note: From the Book of Mazarbul, so the killing of Oin dates to Balin's colony (T.A. 2989-2994). The corpus file prints the acute of 'Óin' on a line of its own, so the joined text reads 'Oin'. Its body is never seen: 'Out from the water a long sinuous tentacle had crawled; it was pale-green and luminous and wet' (t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:14190), and 'Many coiling arms seized the doors on either side' (t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:14207). It has a title, not a personal name.

epithet: the Great Willow of the Withywindle
end: Not slain: Tom Bombadil sang him back to sleep and made him let his prisoners go.
q: Moving constantly in and out of his talk was Old Man Willow, and Frodo learned now enough to content him, indeed more than enough, for it was not comfortable lore.
note: Description at t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:6373: 'But none were more dangerous than the Great Willow: his heart was rotten, but his strength was green; and he was cunning, and a master of winds, and his song and thought ran through the woods on both sides of the river.' Tom names and rebukes him at t1_c_the_lord_of_the_rings.txt:5934-5947.

epithet: the skin-changer / bear-man of the Anduin vales
end: Not stated in The Hobbit; he became a chieftain of the Beornings.
q: He is a skin-changer. He changes his skin: sometimes he is a huge black bear, sometimes he is a great strong black-haired man with huge arms and a great beard.
note: 'he is under no enchantment but his own' (t1_b_the_hobbit_or_there_and_back_again.txt:3335). Gandalf gives two accounts of his descent — from the ancient bears of the mountains, or from the first Men — and favours the second. Borderline for this register: a person who becomes a beast rather than a beast with a name.
